all_reviews.review: Bingo

all_reviews.title: 28 Years Later: The Bone Temple
all_reviews.review:
Fun, mega-gnarly, dong-filled

Ralph Fiennes and Jack O'Connell carried the energy throughout. I was a bit surprised they sidelined the kid character, since he was really good in the first, but maybe he will pop off again in the third installment?

all_reviews.title: Before Sunset
all_reviews.review: Men will literally write a best-selling novel about the most romantic night of their life in Vienna with a stranger they met on the train instead of going to therapy

all_reviews.title: Inside Llewyn Davis
all_reviews.review:
The movie that made me realize I had become cat-pilled.

Coen brother's movies are so reliably wonderful. They are filled with the best kind of things films can do; make you laugh in interesting ways, make you sad in interesting ways, make you think about the meaning behind really small moments. I get to the end of one of their films and thing "yeah this is why I like movies"

all_reviews.title: No Other Choice
all_reviews.review:
The usual Park Chan-wook goodness:
- Symbolism maxxing (a decaying tooth, bugs eating thing alive, pig slaughter, forcing the bend of a bonsai tree with wire until it snaps, etc etc etc)
- Truly S-tier creativity in terms of match cuts, cross dissolves, camera locations and camera movements (the digging scenes, my god)
- Good comedy and good fucked up shit

However, like Decision to Leave, it was verging on incoherence at points and that disconnected me a bit from the story and characters.

The Handmaiden is still peak for me. It has all the good stuff I mentioned while also maintaining emotional connection to the characters and executing a legible (though convoluted) narrative.

all_reviews.title: 12 Angry Men
all_reviews.review:
Bechdel lives in fear of this movie.

It was between this and My Cousin Vinny to get Sarah prepped for her jury duty tomorrow.

all_reviews.title: The New Yorker at 100
all_reviews.review:
Instead of a review, check out my erudite-maxxing watch setup instead: https://imgur.com/a/29Z4QEm (haven’t read any of them btw).

This was fun because I like the New Yorker and I love the folks that work there getting airtime, but it had nothing on Very Semi-Serious.

all_reviews.title: Jay Kelly
all_reviews.review:
Anodyne, saccharine, but it goes down easy

Lost half a star from that god awful scene between Dern and Sandler, how does that dialogue end up in the final cut of a Baumbach movie?

2025 “power of art” / “bad dad” triple feature with Hamnet and Sentimental Value.

all_reviews.title: Marty Supreme
all_reviews.review: Intentional, relentless, aggressive

all_reviews.title: Anchorman: The Legend of Ron Burgundy
all_reviews.review: The next stop on Sarah’s movie education tour

all_reviews.title: Avatar: Fire and Ash
all_reviews.review: For the record, if the sexy goth Na'vi warrior gave me space coke and asked me to be her sex slave I'd say yes. Just for the record.

all_reviews.title: It Was Just an Accident
all_reviews.review:
The underlying moral debate of this film is really interesting, but I think the delivery of that dialogue needed more character arc or plot or something else. By the end I was a little tired of everyone yelling the same questions over and over again.

On the positive note, the dark humor was great and the ending was amazing and almost, but not quite totally redeeming of my earlier boredom.

all_reviews.title: Train Dreams
all_reviews.review: 2025 has been a great year for smaller, grounded, emotionally moving flicks (Sentimental Value, Hamnet, Sorry Baby, to name a few others). Who doesn't like sitting in a dark room (or plane) with strangers (or friends, or alone) and getting a little dirt in your eyes that you have to wipe away for a minute.

all_reviews.title: Wake Up Dead Man
all_reviews.review:
I could watch a Rian Johnson mystery every single night of my life. Keep em coming!

P.S. Netflix pls don't kill movies thx

all_reviews.title: Nickel Boys
all_reviews.review:
I spent a lot of the movie deciding if the first-person camera was a distracting conceit or something fundamental to telling the story and I landed a bit mixed.

It got be thinking about strong camera constraints in general (oners, bottle movies, etc etc etc) and the balance of artifice vs genuine narrative device. I asked that question of other movies I have liked like Birdman, Enter the Void, 12 Angry Men, Presence and how IMO they work a bit better with their chosen tricks.

Final decision - this story could have been told just as well (maybe even better) without the strong first-person constraint, but it will certainly be more memorable because of it. Anyway... 3.5 stars.

all_reviews.title: Michael Clayton
all_reviews.review:
One of the few movies you watch then say "they don't make em like they used to" and actually be spot on.

It's the accumulation of a thousand of small, perfectly-made choices that elevate above its trope-y genre.

all_reviews.title: Juror #2
all_reviews.review:
A movie that asks "what would you do?" when the answer is blindingly obvious: just lie and shirk off jury duty just like everyone else.

There might exist a particular mood I could have been in where I liked this a bit more, but I was not in that hypothetical mood when I watched it. I couldn't deal with the atrocious dialogue or the never-ending stream of plot holes. They kept hanging lampshades on the implausibilities but that somehow made it worse (e.g. "oh we couldn't possibly have a mistrial on this extremely high-profile case we're simply toooo busy").

all_reviews.title: Boiling Point
all_reviews.review:
That one episode of The Bear but make it 90 minutes long and also Scouse

Bonus half star because it isn't every day I discover a new example of Cockney rhyming slang (henry -> henry the eighth -> eight ball)

all_reviews.title: Wicked: For Good
all_reviews.review:
Just about the same amount worse than the first movie as the second act of the musical was worse than the first act of the musical.

Uphill battle for this to be good:
- no novelty effect of seeing this world realized in a film setting
- shorter source material so they have to stretch it and add filler
- fewer banger songs
- darker and less fun plot

No Good Deed was peak though.

all_reviews.title: Hamnet
all_reviews.review:
If you have read my review of Pride & Prejudice then you know some of my thoughts on adaptations.

In general I look for two things. I want an adaptation to stay true to the tone & themes of the original. I also want it to add to the original in ways that are only possible in the form factor of the adaptation (a movie adaptation of a book should be more cinematic, a book adaptation of a movie should be more literary, a play adaptation of a novel should be more theatrical, etc etc etc for all permutations).

This adaptation was true to the tone of the book throughout, but it took till the last twenty minute to make me feel like it was doing something moving in a cinematic way that a book could never accomplish. Once it got there, it was spectacular.

I would have enjoyed the first half more if it had stuck to the non-linear narrative of the novel (you know I'm a sucker for non-linear narrative).

My short reviews usually do better on here (brevity is the soul of wit, after all) but sometime ya just have to rant.

P.S. for a recent adaptation that I think broke from the theme of the source material and it pissed me off see my review of Frankenstein.

all_reviews.title: Sentimental Value
all_reviews.review:
Another understated and evocative drama from Trier. While it didn't quite have the sustained energy or personal resonance of Worst Person, the emotional crux of the film still get me like 🥺😢

Waiting for the digital release so I can re-watch the monologues back to back.

all_reviews.title: Young Frankenstein
all_reviews.review: The only Frankenstein adaptation for me, thank you very much

all_reviews.title: Frankenstein
all_reviews.review:
This adaptation focusses on monster == misunderstood and good, humans == bad and evil (except for the monster-loving chick), which is incredibly on-brand for Del Toro.

IMO, Frankenstein is so much more interesting when the themes are monster == complex creature musing about life and love, humans == complex creatures musing about life and love.

Also, a lot has already been said about the "you’re the monster" line, but jfc I wanted to call up Guillermo and say "my guy, anyone who is voluntarily watching a 2.5 hour adaptation of Frankenstein is literary enough to pick up on the theme here"

My face when I realize I haven't really loved a Del Toro movie in over a decade 🙁☹️😩

all_reviews.title: Materialists
all_reviews.review:
This review may contain spoilers.

A full star of the review is attributed to the Chekhov's leg extension reveal and the subsequent line "it's hard to think it's not about the legs right now"

I truly believe that this could have been 4.5 stars if Dakota Johnson and Chris Evans were replaced with Emma Stone and Josh O'Conner.

Alt review: OMG Celine is right, poor people deserve to fall in love too

This was so small and so so good. Seriously, the dialog was so good I could listen to this movie as a podcast. It was naturalistic, awkward, profound, aching, wonderful.

all_reviews.title: Wild Dark Shore
all_reviews.author: Charlotte McConaghy
all_reviews.review:
I'm done reading climate fiction unless it's written by Richard Powers.

Alt review: it's a thin line between a good book and just a fine book (derogatory).

Double alt review: I am no hater of the Poor Communication Kills trope, but like, come on fam

all_reviews.title: Vigil
all_reviews.author: George Saunders
all_reviews.review:
With recent Saunders (this and Liberation Day) I found myself craving more of the intense novelty that permeates his earlier collections. This book shares the same conceit as Lincoln in the Bardo, which is a great conceit for exploring themes on morality and humanity, but loses some of the freshness of reading about a limbo-world for the first time.

That being said, this book still delivers all the good Saunders-stuff that has him as my favorite contemporary author. He portrays his characters with a blunt sincerity that truly understands them without judgement.

Recency bias, but I found myself thinking about Joachim Trier and how his movies are filled with real, three-dimensional people instead of archetypes of good and bad. Same with Saunders (except sometimes the real people are ghosts

all_reviews.title: The Correspondent
all_reviews.author: Virginia      Evans
all_reviews.review: Easy reading, creative structure, well-wrought main character, funny at points, sad at points, what is not to like here??

all_reviews.title: Slouching Towards Bethlehem
all_reviews.author: Joan Didion
all_reviews.review: Candidly, I struggle with collections of essays (which is surprising because I love collections of short stories). Not doing myself any favors by reading this late at night, I would sometimes catch my eyes just scanning the words. However, for the essays that caught me, and there were quite a few, I was loving the prose and structure. In particular, "On Keeping a Notebook" and "Goodbye to All That" resonated deeply and personally (having kept a journal for years, and having had a dreamlike 6 months of living in nyc in my early twenties).

all_reviews.title: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone (Harry Potter, #1)
all_reviews.author: J.K. Rowling
all_reviews.review:
Full-cast Dolby audiobook for commuting / solo workouts.

This was a really fun new addition to the many many many ways to consume the Harry Potter world. Shall I list them all? The original books, the Jim Dale audiobooks, the Stephen Fry audiobooks, the movies, dramione smut, the old PC video games, the newer Switch video game, the future HBO series.

And now this, a full-cast audiobook with mind-boggling 3D sound effects and great voice acting (some of whom you will recognize, Matthew Macfadyen so good!)

all_reviews.title: Pale Fire
all_reviews.author: Vladimir Nabokov
all_reviews.review:
Wow this was l i t e r a r y, and it was just at the outer limit of my attention span outside of an academic setting (don't get many of those these days do I). I was only able to get through thanks to the long break from work and work related stress. I'm glad I had the chance because in the end this was a very rewarding puzzle to unpack and enjoy.

At some points I wished to have this in print format for easier flipping back and forth, but I appreciated Kindle's X-Ray feature for the first time ever, and I very much relied on its dictionary (was looking up about one word per page).

With all the cross-references and intertextual links, by the time I finished I felt like I'd read the entire book three times.

all_reviews.author: Gillian Flynn
all_reviews.review: Solid B-tier thriller

all_reviews.title: Barbarians at the Gate: The Fall of RJR Nabisco

all_reviews.review: Just like Succession except it’s a 600 page book and it all actually happened

all_reviews.review:
Ngl the bacchanal sounded pretty lit. I'm going to call up my Great Books TA from Michigan and lodge a complain.

3.5 / 5 stars: fun to read but got a bit repetitive, draggy and aimless in the back half.

all_reviews.title: Kafka on the Shore
all_reviews.author: Haruki Murakami
all_reviews.review: This was a really strange book... good thing I like strange books!

all_reviews.title: The Great Gatsby
all_reviews.author: F. Scott Fitzgerald
all_reviews.review:
Audio book for the new commute. Banger after banger of quotable lines that have been seared in my head since high school.

Favorite deeper cut line from this re-read re-listen:
"What'll we do with ourselves this afternoon," cried Daisy, "and the day after that, and the next thirty years?"

all_reviews.title: The Last Life
all_reviews.author: Claire Messud
all_reviews.review: Smooth reading, but the lack of a driving narrative will make this forgettable for me, in the end

all_reviews.title: The Elements of Investing
all_reviews.author: Burton G. Malkiel
all_reviews.review: Funemployment is as good a time as ever to remember the fundamentals!

all_reviews.title: Frankenstein
all_reviews.author: Mary Wollstonecraft Shelley
all_reviews.review:
Missed this classic in High School (shame on you Shaker!). It was absolutely nothing like I expected it would be based off pop culture. I was waiting for mobs, Fritz/Igor, and "it's alive" before realizing all that stuff was added by later movie adaptations.

Instead I got stories with in stories told in beautiful language and varying structure on themes of ambition, science, love, etc etc etc. Anyway, it was great.

Shoutout to Guillermo del Toro for the catalyst to read.

all_reviews.title: James
all_reviews.author: Percival Everett
all_reviews.review: I didn't really vibe with the writing style - too much telling and not enough showing. There were some bits in there I did enjoy (the reclaimed code switching, small philosophies on god, huckleberry ret cons) but in the end this was a book I was reading just to get to the end so I could read a different book.

all_reviews.title: Sunrise on the Reaping (The Hunger Games, #0.5)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins
all_reviews.review:
This is an example of a prequel that actually causes a character to lose depth. Haymitch was a fully three-dimensional character in the original trilogy, with a backstory and that was shown, not told, and understood through dialog and choices.

This prequel was a cameo-jammed, overly retconned, unnecessary retelling of a story that packed a bigger punch in less than ten pages of Catching Fire.

The only saving grace - even though I complained about it a sentence ago - is that each cameo did make me squeal "omg I KNOW them."

all_reviews.title: Station Eleven
all_reviews.author: Emily St. John Mandel
all_reviews.review:
Re-reading this post-pandy hits a lot harder. The chapters that describe the fallout from the flu went from being interesting sci-fi to fridge-horror what-ifs about the real pandemic.

Such a solid work of literary-ish sci-fi. I could see someone criticizing this book for having an anti-climatic ending, but they would have missed the the fact that it was all about the journey.

ORIGINAL 2017 REVIEW BELOW

I'll always love a well executed, deeply intertwined, mosaic piece.

Read this very shortly after reading The Road, and the astounding differences in tone were fun to think about.

all_reviews.title: The Seven Moons of Maali Almeida
all_reviews.author: Shehan Karunatilaka
all_reviews.review:
This was a creative, kafkaesque take on the bardo, and naturally it reminded me of my favorite George Saunder's novel, but MAN I did not connect with the writing style. The second person threw me, I found it tonally confused, and my god the acronyms just wouldn't stop.

On the plus side I learned a lot about Sri Lanka and TAMIL came up in a nyt crossword while I was reading it so there's that I guess.

all_reviews.title: Playground
all_reviews.author: Richard Powers
all_reviews.review:
Absolutely wonderful. I liked this significantly better than The Overstory and I already liked that one a lot. It took a little to get going, but once the characters and story were established it started to hum. Like his other books, this has themes of environmentalism, technology, friendship, love, and family. For a lot of other novels that would feel like too much, but Powers is so good at weaving themes and meanings into the narrative that it never felt like this was overreaching.

I am a big fan of meta-review about "cinematic moments" I had sitting in movies theaters and "literary moments" I had reading something wherever I was reading it. Well, this gave me a big one as I stayed up to finish it alone in a ski lease in Tahoe. I am putting it up there with a few other notable moments like:
- Finishing 100 Years on a boat with my family
- Reading book 7 of HP for the first time curled up in my childhood bedroom
- The end of And the Mountains Echoed on a ferry to Martha's Vineyard

all_reviews.title: Onyx Storm (The Empyrean, #3)
all_reviews.author: Rebecca Yarros
all_reviews.review:
I think if I tried to list out all my complaints they would be as long as the book itself. But I will try to list a few!
- so many fucking names
- absolutely ZERO character development
- bizarre pacing, esp during scenes that should have been intense, everything just felt... flat
- filler plot that accomplishes just about nothing
- infuriatingly repetitive conversations between main characters

The only reason this gets 2 stars and not 1 is the hangover effect from the first book. I still like the world that was built and am desperate for more crumbs of information about it.

Where were you when the era of dragon smut died?

all_reviews.title: Manacled
all_reviews.author: SenLinYu
all_reviews.review:
Yes that's right I started 2025 off by reading smutting HP fanfic

This was OK, it had a good premise but started dragging in pace about halfway through and never recovered

all_reviews.title: Orbital
all_reviews.author: Samantha Harvey
all_reviews.review: I can understand someone not liking this book, but I did enjoyed its poetic, non-fictionish style. The plot is barely existent but the writing and multicultural space-musings kept me engaged throughout

all_reviews.title: We Have Always Lived in the Castle
all_reviews.author: Shirley Jackson
all_reviews.review: I did not like this very much - but it broke my multi month reading dry spell so it has that going for it

all_reviews.title: Bewilderment
all_reviews.author: Richard Powers
all_reviews.review: My favorite parts were the parts about the planets

all_reviews.title: Sapiens: A Brief History of Humankind
all_reviews.author: Yuval Noah Harari
all_reviews.review: Tried to read this about a decade ago and did not like it. Tried it again and enjoyed it more! Didn't agree with all the hot takes, but book is well structured and got me thinking about things I wasn't thinking about before so that is good. By the end I was happy to finish it so I can go back to reading fiction

all_reviews.title: Lincoln in the Bardo
all_reviews.author: George Saunders
all_reviews.review:
Told with an intensely unique style, this book provides an amazing meditation on death, grief, parenthood, and the afterlife.

Re-read it after 7 years and it still hits

all_reviews.title: The Hero of Ages (Mistborn, #3)
all_reviews.author: Brandon Sanderson

all_reviews.author: Brandon Sanderson

all_reviews.title: Mistborn: The Final Empire (Mistborn, #1)
all_reviews.author: Brandon Sanderson

all_reviews.title: The Arsonists' City
all_reviews.author: Hala Alyan
all_reviews.review: The only positive thing I can say about this book is that I learned a little about Beirut and Damascus. Besides that it was awful. The characters were insipid, uncharismatic assholes and the plot was just plodding along without intention. I swear there are more interesting plot developments than infidelity but I guess this author hasn't figured them out yet.

all_reviews.title: In Cold Blood
all_reviews.author: Truman Capote
all_reviews.review:
Gripping, chilling, humanizing, dehumanizing. I guess I like true crime now.

I have a friend who says she has read this book 6 times - should I be worried?

all_reviews.title: Klara and the Sun
all_reviews.author: Kazuo Ishiguro
all_reviews.review: Engaging read, because I absolutely love Ishiguro's style, but not as moving or memorable as Never Let Me Go or Remains of the Day. It felt a bit emotionally flatter and, the surrounding world was less realized.

all_reviews.title: Machines like Me
all_reviews.author: Ian McEwan
all_reviews.review: There were some interesting ideas tossed around, but in the end I found it a bit unfocussed and annoying. Maybe all the talk of P=NP was just too close to home for me... I had flashbacks to my introductory Computer Science theory course at umich

all_reviews.title: Defending Jacob
all_reviews.author: William Landay
all_reviews.review: Fun crime slash court room thriller

all_reviews.title: North Woods
all_reviews.author: Daniel       Mason
all_reviews.review:
Here is a little list of books that, if you liked them a lot, I think you will like this book a lot:
· The Overstory
· Lincoln in the Bardo
· Cloud Cuckoo Land

Those three books have all been my favorite in the year that I read them so naturally, I loved this book. It is a sweeping mosaic novel that is grand in scope and varied in structure. I loved almost all of the sub-stories (with one notable exception that I didn't vibe with). In the end I found that all the connective tissue of the story really held together for a cohesive whole. 10/10 will be re reading one day.

all_reviews.title: Recursion
all_reviews.author: Blake Crouch
all_reviews.review:
Candy Sci-Fi Beach Read (same category as Dark Matter)

Super loose spoilers below for the first 1/3 of the book.

The writing is passable, and serves as a fine platter to serve up some heady, mind-bending time travel ideas. You know it's a good one when you're out there diagramming timelines on the back of a napkin to explain things.

Definitely got some Eternal Sunshine vibes from the beginning.

For Dark Matter, I said I wanted to see a movie adaptation directed by Denis Villeneuve or Ryan Johnson. For this one, I think Shane Carruth is the only director that could handle the complexities.

all_reviews.title: And Then There Were None
all_reviews.author: Agatha Christie
all_reviews.review: Fun stuff, not as much of a banger as Murder on Orient Express though

all_reviews.title: The Marriage Portrait
all_reviews.author: Maggie O'Farrell
all_reviews.review:
Pale shadow of how good Hamnet was.

The writing was nice, but the pacing and plot felt flat and low-stakes. Also... kinda predictable!

all_reviews.title: I Who Have Never Known Men
all_reviews.author: Jacqueline Harpman
all_reviews.review: An absolute gem of surreal speculative fiction.

all_reviews.title: The Overstory
all_reviews.author: Richard Powers
all_reviews.review:
This has a lot going for it
- enormous, resounding scope
- trees
- structural complexity
- more trees
- thematic complexity
- did I mention the trees?

I felt a little far away from the book at points, that honestly might have been because I was not in a situation where I could give it full attention. This is a book that needs your full attention.

Here's to starting 2024 with a harder one, gonna go read a bunch of easy ones to buff the stats

all_reviews.title: How Big Things Get Done: The Surprising Factors That Determine the Fate of Every Project, from Home Renovations to Space Exploration and Everything In Between
all_reviews.author: Bent Flyvbjerg
all_reviews.review: We

all_reviews.title: The Handmaid’s Tale (The Handmaid's Tale, #1)
all_reviews.author: Margaret Atwood
all_reviews.review: Re reading this in a post-Trump world hits different

all_reviews.title: Good Strategy Bad Strategy: The Difference and Why It Matters
all_reviews.author: Richard P. Rumelt
all_reviews.review: I'll let you know how helpful this book was after the next OKR planning season

all_reviews.title: The Bell Jar
all_reviews.author: Sylvia Plath
all_reviews.review: This one gave me a load of fridge horror after I read up on insulin shock therapy after finishing

all_reviews.title: Mockingjay (The Hunger Games, #3)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins
all_reviews.review:
I guess it is time to stop re reading YA fantasy to buff my yearly stats

I forgot how frustrated I was with the end of this one until I re read it.

all_reviews.title: Catching Fire (The Hunger Games, #2)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins
all_reviews.review: Back in the Hunger Games Universe and loving it

all_reviews.title: The Da Vinci Code (Robert Langdon, #2)
all_reviews.author: Dan    Brown

all_reviews.title: Iron Flame (The Empyrean, #2)
all_reviews.author: Rebecca Yarros
all_reviews.review: Not quite as novel as the first since there wasn't as much world-building to do, but still a fun read. Some aspects got a little tiresome; aka the romance and smut

all_reviews.title: The Hunger Games (The Hunger Games, #1)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins
all_reviews.review:
Holding pattern while Sarah reads Iron Flame / prepping for the new movie / buffing my 2023 reading stats

I think that the Hunger Games movies might be the most well cast YA-turned-cinematic-universe of all time

all_reviews.title: Project Hail Mary
all_reviews.author: Andy Weir
all_reviews.review: His writing is annoying, the characters are annoying, but hey, the science shit is fun enough to keep me on the train. I still think The Egg is the best thing Andy Weir has written

all_reviews.title: Fourth Wing (The Empyrean, #1)
all_reviews.author: Rebecca Yarros
all_reviews.review:
Harry Potter meets the Hunger Games, but also it's sexy

Alt review; if Sarah reads fantasy dragon smut, I read fantasy dragon smut

all_reviews.title: Tom Lake
all_reviews.author: Ann Patchett
all_reviews.review: Simple, small-scale and cozy to read

all_reviews.title: The Silent Patient
all_reviews.author: Alex Michaelides
all_reviews.review: Page turner! Nothing special about the writing or characters, but the plot was such a fun ride I couldn't stop reading.

all_reviews.title: The Death of Ivan Ilych
all_reviews.author: Leo Tolstoy
all_reviews.review: Maybe this is the gateway novella I needed to finally finish reading Anna Karenina

all_reviews.title: The Remains of the Day
all_reviews.author: Kazuo Ishiguro
all_reviews.review:
I really love Ishiguro's prose. It wanders around and around, but manages to stay completely focussed. Every digression either forwards the plot or adds depth and nuance to a character. He manages to slowly reveal new plot points at an extremely measured pace through flashbacks and memories. I remember feeling the same way reading Never Let Me Go.

This book was full of interesting musings on memory, goodness, dignity, human connection, and love. Now I'll go watch the movie.

all_reviews.title: Dark Matter
all_reviews.author: Blake Crouch
all_reviews.review:
(beach read)^2

candy sci fi

I would rather have watched this as a movie adaptation directed by Denis Villeneuve or Ryan Johnson

all_reviews.title: Four Thousand Weeks: Time Management for Mortals
all_reviews.author: Oliver Burkeman
all_reviews.review:
"Hmm what a good point, we do all rush through life" I thought to myself as I rushed to finish this book, check it off my list, and get back to work /s

I approached this with hesitancy, because I hate pop psych self help books, but it was actually good! Some parts were freshman-smoking-weed in a dorm philosophy like "we are time" and some parts were more targeted thoughts on time management. There were enough interesting points in it that kept me reading to the end

all_reviews.title: Trust
all_reviews.author: Hernan Diaz
all_reviews.review:
This took a while to really catch me, but man it was the read. The threads of the novel are perfectly woven together for a really satisfying conclusion. Themes of music and money and memory and storytelling are blended to profound effect.

I love books within books and stories within stories and this novel had a lot of that. The writing was fantastic and I learned a lot of new words along the way.

all_reviews.title: Tomorrow, and Tomorrow, and Tomorrow
all_reviews.author: Gabrielle Zevin
all_reviews.review:
Super fun beach read page turner! Made me think a lot about my game design capstone project back at Michigan. This was a very smooth read, and I really enjoyed some of the structural creativities throughout (writing from the perspective of a video game, second person present for a chapter, etc etc etc). Some of the dialogue felt cheesy, but other parts felt really authentic and insightful.

My only true complaint is that it felt a little thesaurus-y at parts, with random SAT words smooshed in there. That being said, I am sure that same complaint could be leveled at me... so I am not trying to throw too many stones here.

In the morning, from bed, I reached for my kindle instead of my phone to keep reading, which has not happened in a really long time.

all_reviews.title: Beneath a Scarlet Sky
all_reviews.author: Mark T. Sullivan
all_reviews.review:
Nice historical fiction! Definitely fun to read this on a honeymoon to northern Italy. Was a bit disappointed after doing a little research in the real Pino Lella, but hey it’s historical fiction right

Read this with Sarah and everyday we would discuss and the main point was “shit we really know nothing about WWII history.” That’s on us for both skipping APUSH in high school

all_reviews.title: Morning Star (Red Rising Saga, #3)
all_reviews.author: Pierce Brown
all_reviews.review: Still a good read, but a little less cohesive than the second book and a lot less cohesive than the first. This most likely will be the end of my journey with this series.

all_reviews.title: Golden Son (Red Rising Saga, #2)
all_reviews.author: Pierce Brown
all_reviews.review:
I think the pace dropped a tiny bit from the first book, but this one still kept me pretty locked in the whole way. Good sci fi fun

I am 9 books in the hole for my 2023 reading goals. Think I can make a historic comeback on my honeymoon??

all_reviews.title: Red Rising (Red Rising Saga, #1)
all_reviews.author: Pierce Brown
all_reviews.review: Very fun, plot-driven sci fi. This exists somewhere between Ender's Game and Hunger Games, with characters that are a bit less empathetic. I have finally broken my 2023 reading dry spell

all_reviews.title: Our Missing Hearts
all_reviews.author: Celeste Ng
all_reviews.review: Fool me twice, shame on me. Don't know why I tried to read another Celeste Ng book after I hated Little Fires Everywhere. I won't be trying again.

all_reviews.title: The Manager's Path: A Guide for Tech Leaders Navigating Growth and Change
all_reviews.author: Camille Fournier
all_reviews.review: Pretty good, some nice points

all_reviews.title: Sea of Tranquility
all_reviews.author: Emily St. John Mandel
all_reviews.review: Nice light sci fi. I thought it was a little predictable, but I still had fun on the ride

all_reviews.title: This Is How You Lose the Time War
all_reviews.author: Amal El-Mohtar
all_reviews.review: Somehow both predictable and unpredictable at the same time. By that I mean that the overall arc was a little basic and unsurprising, but the structure, details, and language continued to delight me in creative ways. In the end it was quick, fun, and I have thought about it a bit since reading, so I recommend for anyone who wants a little dose of heady, time-travel sci fi!

all_reviews.title: Liberation Day
all_reviews.author: George Saunders
all_reviews.review:
George Saunders just doesn't miss. Excellent collection of stories.

Personal favorite was My House, but all of them were creative, profound, insane, funny, etc etc etc. You know, the usual Saunders stuff.

Some stories contained echoes of his earlier works (Liberation Day -> The Semplica-Girl Diaries and Ghoul -> Pastoralia), but all managed to inject enough new-ness that they didn't feel derivative at all.

all_reviews.title: Tender Is the Flesh
all_reviews.author: Agustina Bazterrica
all_reviews.review:
I was fooled by a tik tok book influencer into reading this, and so I am to blame for having to suffer through it. The premise was pretty dark, but that is about the only good thing this book has going for it.

Not sure if it was bad writing or bad translation or both, but it was nearly impossible to palate. At some points I actually put on my tin foil hat and wondered if I was reading a book written by a computer...

all_reviews.title: Gregor and the Code of Claw (Underland Chronicles, #5)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins
all_reviews.review: Re-reading a five book YA series is a great way to catch up on the Goodreads yearly reading challenge

all_reviews.title: Gregor and the Marks of Secret (Underland Chronicles, #4)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins
all_reviews.review:
I don't think I realized how dark this was when I read this as a kid...

I was surprised by how vividly some scenes sat in my head even though I read this over a decade ago

all_reviews.title: Gregor and the Curse of the Warmbloods (Underland Chronicles, #3)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins

all_reviews.title: Gregor and the Prophecy of Bane (Underland Chronicles, #2)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins

all_reviews.title: Gregor the Overlander (Underland Chronicles, #1)
all_reviews.author: Suzanne Collins
all_reviews.review: Nothing like quarantine for an excuse to re-read some cozy young adult fiction. If you like Hunger Games and haven't read this series, I would highly recommend! It is a bit more juvenile, but is still packed with twists and turns and fast paced action. Good one to pick up next time you or your fiancee has Covid

all_reviews.title: The Topeka School
all_reviews.author: Ben Lerner

all_reviews.title: Cloud Cuckoo Land
all_reviews.author: Anthony Doerr
all_reviews.review: Astounding in scope, this mosaic-novel is essentially a love letter to the power of stories. It traces five seemingly unrelated story arcs across hundreds of years, but as you expect the narrative threads delicately start to weave into each other in very subtle and thought provoking ways. The novel blends historical fiction with sci-fi with contemporary fiction but manages to be completely cohesive. I have a lot more to say about this, but I will leave it out of the review. Just go read it.

all_reviews.title: Upgrade
all_reviews.author: Blake Crouch
all_reviews.review: Not nearly as good as Recursion or Dark Matter. Not worth the read IMO

all_reviews.title: Hyperion (Hyperion Cantos, #1)
all_reviews.author: Dan Simmons
all_reviews.review: Essentially a collection of six short stories within the structured narrative of a pilgrims journey. All of the stories were thought provoking, some more than others. I liked the last two the best

all_reviews.title: Israel: A Simple Guide to the Most Misunderstood Country on Earth
all_reviews.author: Noa Tishby
all_reviews.review: Super biased, but also informative for someone like me who previously knew very very little about this hot potato of a subject

all_reviews.title: Waiting for Godot
all_reviews.author: Samuel Beckett
all_reviews.review:
I read this after seeing the movie Drive My Car.

Very good, but I probably needed to read it in a class to fully appreciate the themes and symbols.

all_reviews.title: Kotlin in Action
all_reviews.author: Dmitry Jemerov
all_reviews.review: No longer a C++ purist!

all_reviews.title: Hamnet
all_reviews.author: Maggie O'Farrell
all_reviews.review: Fantastic book. This novel takes a handful of historical facts about Shakespeare's life and weaves them into a magical tale about family, grief, and human connection. In premise, it reminded me a lot of one of my favorite books: Lincoln in the Bardo. I highly recommend that to anyone who likes Hamnet.

all_reviews.title: Radical Candor: Be a Kickass Boss Without Losing Your Humanity
all_reviews.author: Kim Malone Scott
all_reviews.review: Good read for anyone in tech, irrespective of role. Lots of the content might be obvious, but there are definitely some interesting viewpoints and techniques. There were also some aspects that I had understood subconsciously, but it was good to name them and pick them apart in this book.

all_reviews.title: The Checklist Manifesto: How to Get Things Right
all_reviews.author: Atul Gawande
all_reviews.review: Interesting read. Covers topics from surgery to construction to aviation

all_reviews.title: The Ocean at the End of the Lane
all_reviews.author: Neil Gaiman
all_reviews.review: Great little gem of a book. Dark, fairytale-like sci fi

all_reviews.title: Macbeth
all_reviews.author: William Shakespeare
all_reviews.review: Re-read this to prepare for the new movie, it still slaps.

all_reviews.title: Ready Player Two (Ready Player One, #2)
all_reviews.author: Ernest Cline
all_reviews.review: Not good

all_reviews.title: Ready Player One (Ready Player One, #1)
all_reviews.author: Ernest Cline
all_reviews.review:
Decided to reread this now that Meta is making OASIS real.

Also I want to read the sequel. I love easy sci fi.

all_reviews.title: The Redemption of Time (The Three-Body Problem Series Book 4)
all_reviews.author: Baoshu
all_reviews.review: I found the story behind this fanfiction-turned-canon paraquel interesting. Unfortunately, the content of the book was way too out there for me.

all_reviews.title: Death's End (Remembrance of Earth’s Past, #3)
all_reviews.author: Liu Cixin
all_reviews.review: Not as tight as the first two in the series, but still fun to read and chock full of interesting sci fi concepts.

all_reviews.title: The Dark Forest (Remembrance of Earth’s Past, #2)
all_reviews.author: Liu Cixin
all_reviews.review: Heady, hard sci fi. Very fun to read

all_reviews.title: The Three-Body Problem (Remembrance of Earth’s Past, #1)
all_reviews.author: Liu Cixin
all_reviews.review:
Fun, gripping, hard sci-fi.

The title game-within-a-book reminded me of Terry Pratchett in the best way possible.

all_reviews.title: Someone Who Will Love You in All Your Damaged Glory
all_reviews.author: Raphael Bob-Waksberg

all_reviews.title: One Hundred Years of Solitude
all_reviews.author: Gabriel García Márquez

all_reviews.title: The Magician's Land (The Magicians, #3)
all_reviews.author: Lev Grossman
all_reviews.review: Just a bit less engaging than the seconds book (which itself was just a bit less engaging than the first book). Worth a read just to get some closure on the trilogy's built world, but much less memorable.

all_reviews.title: The Magician King (The Magicians, #2)
all_reviews.author: Lev Grossman
all_reviews.review:
Not as good as the first book, but still good. In my review of the first one I called it a cross between Harry Potter and Narnia. To follow that template, I would call this one a mix between Narnia and Lord of the Rings. We follow the heroes on a quest to attain 7 keys, while exploring more deeply the fantasy world that was set up in the first book.

This book also gives a deeper backstory for a character from the first installment in a series of alternating chapters with the main plot. I found myself more captivated by the secondary plot, and was reminded of the second Eragon book where I was more engrossed in the Roran Stronghammer subplot than the main storyline.

Worth a read if you liked The Magicians a lot, but that being said, it was definitely a noticeable notch down in terms of writing quality.

all_reviews.title: The Magicians (The Magicians, #1)
all_reviews.author: Lev Grossman
all_reviews.review:
Imagine if Harry Potter and the Chronicles of Narnia had a child, but that child was depressed, mopey, and a little horny; maybe that child would look something like this book. Replete with lampshade-hangy references to to former parent and suspiciously devoid of those to the latter, The Magicians sits somewhere in between the two works.

It starts by exploring some college-age students in a magic university and transitions into an intense fantasy world quest novel, all while playing with adult themes like drugs and sex and big questions like "what if all your dreams came true but you were still unhappy?"

I love this book and have read it a few times. That being said, I can understand not liking this book if you don't like stories with characters that are hard to like (at least at first). I am re-reading now so I can remember it all before reading the third book, which I read once, but can't remember much about.

all_reviews.title: Pride and Prejudice
all_reviews.author: Jane Austen
all_reviews.review:
The prose took some getting used to - which is to be expected considering it was written over two hundred years ago - but once I warmed up to the style, I was quite surprised to find this book something of a page turner. There I was, up at one in the morning to find out what would happen next between Elizabeth and her suitor... never would have guessed.

I liked the antiquated words and the use of second definitions (which were probably first definitions back then). I loved the long, winding sentences that only revealed their meaning in the ultimate or penultimate clause, and found myself often needing to re-read a paragraph to fully decode it.

all_reviews.title: The Andromeda Strain
all_reviews.author: Michael Crichton
all_reviews.review: Fast-paced action sci-fi at its finest. The story is very contained: 5 main characters, 5 days, 1 underground lab. Lots of hard science fiction. The book is structured as a write-up that occurred after the events of the novel, which leads to good foreshadowing and clues. Very quick read.

all_reviews.title: Triangulum
all_reviews.author: Masande Ntshanga
all_reviews.review:
I did not like this book at all. The characters felt flat and hard to connect with, the writing was not so good. Since this was sci-fi, all of that would have been ok if the plot was gripping, but it wasn't. I thought it was all over the place and convoluted.

Sorry Nolan

all_reviews.title: The Dinner
all_reviews.author: Herman Koch
all_reviews.review:
Very fast read.

Most manipulative narrator I have read since Lolita...

All in all the story is gripping, but the characters are so sociopathic that I found it hard to immerse fully.

all_reviews.title: Maybe You Should Talk to Someone: A Therapist, Her Therapist, and Our Lives Revealed
all_reviews.author: Lori Gottlieb
all_reviews.review: Interesting read for me because, you know, my mom's a shrink.

all_reviews.title: CivilWarLand in Bad Decline
all_reviews.author: George Saunders
all_reviews.review:
Loved it!

Much rougher, rawer, and darker than his later short stories.

The personal afterthoughts were fascinating, to learn more about his context when writing this first collection of stories.

all_reviews.title: The Mythical Man-Month: Essays on Software Engineering
all_reviews.author: Frederick P. Brooks Jr.
all_reviews.review: Pretty out-of-date, but that is what I get for reading a book about software engineering that was written two decades before I was born...

all_reviews.title: A Swim in a Pond in the Rain
all_reviews.author: George Saunders
all_reviews.review:
I'll never be lucky enough to take a course with George Saunders as the professor, but this book might be the next best thing.

Across 7 short stories, Saunders delivers pedagogical essays and personal afterthoughts that touch on details like form, pattern and pacing, as well as larger ideas like interiory of monologue and contextual meaning (to name only a few).

Too soon to say, but I hope (and believe) that this book has strengthened the "active reading" part of me that is constantly questioning the author's choices from all the way "across the pond".

I'll end with a quote I loved from the prologue: "To study the way we read is to study the way the mind works: the way it evaluates a statement for truth, the way it behaves in relation to another mind (i.e., the writer’s) across space and time."

all_reviews.title: Harry Potter and the Methods of Rationality
all_reviews.author: Eliezer Yudkowsky
all_reviews.review:
This was more fun than it had any right to be. Harry Potter meets Enders Game meets Artemis Fowl.

Really came into its own stride with the first Dementor encounter.

Is this what Methadone feels like?

all_reviews.title: To Be a Man
all_reviews.author: Nicole Krauss
all_reviews.review:
Fantastic collection of short stories.

Personal favorites: Seeing Ershadi & The Husband

all_reviews.title: In a Holidaze
all_reviews.author: Christina Lauren

all_reviews.title: Nothing to See Here
all_reviews.author: Kevin    Wilson

all_reviews.title: The Shining (The Shining, #1)
all_reviews.author: Stephen  King
all_reviews.review: Meh. Go watch the movie instead

all_reviews.title: Leave the World Behind
all_reviews.author: Rumaan Alam
all_reviews.review:
Quasi-literary suspense, pretty fun.

I thought the writing in the first third was noticeably better then the rest, but by that point I was fully engaged in the plot, so ¯\_(ツ)_/¯

all_reviews.title: Circe
all_reviews.author: Madeline Miller
all_reviews.review: This was a FUN read. I knew much of the underlying myth and yet the retelling in Circe was unwaveringly entertaining and gripping. Highly recommend for anyone, regardless of familiarity with the Greek epics at the kernel of the book.

all_reviews.title: A Gentleman in Moscow
all_reviews.author: Amor Towles

all_reviews.title: Less (Arthur Less, #1)
all_reviews.author: Andrew Sean Greer
all_reviews.review:
This book follows the peripatetic Arthur Less on his journey around the wold. It's filled with fun musings on love, middle age ennui, and many scattered Ulysses/Odysseus references.

The style is light and cinematic. Really fun read

all_reviews.title: Masterpieces: The Best Science Fiction of the Twentieth Century
all_reviews.author: Orson Scott Card

all_reviews.title: Life of Pi
all_reviews.author: Yann Martel

all_reviews.title: In Persuasion Nation
all_reviews.author: George Saunders
all_reviews.review:
I like reading earlier stuff by Saunders bc it's cool to see him experimenting with batshit insane stories, threshing out the unique voice that he will eventually refines for collections like 10th of December and books like Lincoln in the Bardo.

That being said, some of the short stories in this one were a little over the top for me...

Loved "I CAN SPEAK!" and "Christmas" broke my heart

all_reviews.title: Here, There and Everywhere: My Life Recording the Music of the Beatles
all_reviews.author: Geoff Emerick
all_reviews.review:
Great look at the Beatles time from a "fly on the wall" perspective.

Lost some energy near the end when he focusses on their solo work

all_reviews.title: Exhalation
all_reviews.author: Ted Chiang
all_reviews.review:
This is fantastic literary sci fi.

Chiang masterfully blends hard science fiction tropes like time travel and parallel universes with lofty philosophical ideas like fatalism, free will, and the power of language. Some stories in the collection are so good they are worth a read and then an immediate re read.

all_reviews.title: Pachinko
all_reviews.author: Min Jin Lee
all_reviews.review: A generational tale with themes of identify, home, and stoicism. Highly recommend

all_reviews.title: The Restaurant at the End of the Universe (The Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, #2)
all_reviews.author: Douglas Adams

all_reviews.title: The Hitchhiker’s Guide to the Galaxy (Hitchhiker's Guide to the Galaxy, #1)
all_reviews.author: Douglas Adams

all_reviews.title: Crossing to Safety
all_reviews.author: Wallace Stegner

all_reviews.title: Buried in the Sky: The Extraordinary Story of the Sherpa Climbers on K2's Deadliest Day
all_reviews.author: Peter Zuckerman

all_reviews.title: Touching the Void: The True Story of One Man's Miraculous Survival
all_reviews.author: Joe Simpson
all_reviews.review: Not as theatrical or grandiose as Into Thin Air, but just as harrowing, if not more so. Be warned, descriptions of the Joe's leg will make you wince.

all_reviews.title: The Beach
all_reviews.author: Alex Garland
all_reviews.review: AWESOME page turner

all_reviews.title: Colorless Tsukuru Tazaki and His Years of Pilgrimage
all_reviews.author: Haruki Murakami

all_reviews.title: Bright Lights, Big City
all_reviews.author: Jay McInerney
all_reviews.review: I guess I like manipulative books

all_reviews.title: Alice’s Adventures in Wonderland / Through the Looking-Glass
all_reviews.author: Lewis Carroll

all_reviews.title: Mort (Discworld, #4; Death, #1)
all_reviews.author: Terry Pratchett

all_reviews.title: Tenth of December
all_reviews.author: George Saunders

all_reviews.title: Exit West
all_reviews.author: Mohsin Hamid
all_reviews.review:
Brilliant and fairytale-like, this story is filled with insightful gems and poetic musings on emigration, religious, relationships, and the beauty of life.

This was one of those books I finished, and had the immediate desire to start again from the beginning.

all_reviews.title: Little Fires Everywhere
all_reviews.author: Celeste Ng
all_reviews.review:
Lots of drama packed into such a small book. While it was engaging and fun, this book won't stick with me. Would be a great beach/candy read though.

The intense Shaker setting was at best nostalgic (she calls out my favorite mural!) and at worst intrusive (not used to my hometown being peered at from an 'outsider' for hundreds of pages).

all_reviews.title: Pastoralia
all_reviews.author: George Saunders

all_reviews.title: 4 3 2 1
all_reviews.author: Paul Auster
all_reviews.review:
This book is engaging and brilliant. A man lives four lives. He grows, the arcs diverge, and meaning can be found within each subplot, as well as in the differences between them. Sometimes he befriends the same people, sometimes he doesn't.

At a young age, all humans play the if game: "But what if I had--? Or maybe if I--? What would my life be if I just--?" This book explores that to the max through 900+ pages answering questions on destiny, fatalism, soulmates, and life.

all_reviews.title: Children of the Mind (Ender's Saga, #4)
all_reviews.author: Orson Scott Card
all_reviews.review:
Upon re-reading this, I had the immediate desire to have kept it shrouded in the nostalgic glow of my favorite childhood sci fi saga. Now I'll always lump it into the "mediocre" pile.

Also, I missed a lot of intense Mormon culture glorification when I read this years ago, and that wasn't exactly my cup of tea.

all_reviews.title: Xenocide (Ender's Saga, #3)
all_reviews.author: Orson Scott Card

all_reviews.title: Speaker for the Dead (Ender's Saga, #2)
all_reviews.author: Orson Scott Card

all_reviews.title: Ender’s Game (Ender's Saga, #1)
all_reviews.author: Orson Scott Card
Ben Lerner
all_reviews.review:
Heady and brilliant, this book is "neither fiction nor nonfiction, but a flickering between them."

The New Yorker story, The Golden Vanity, is the kernel of this work, and if you like the story, then you will like the book.

all_reviews.title: Leaving the Atocha Station
all_reviews.author: Ben Lerner
all_reviews.review:
The main character is anxious, drugged out, a little pretentious, and kind of an asshole... And I still identified with him and enjoyed reading his neurotic thoughts throughout this book.

Not as good as 10:04, but if you liked that you would probably like this.

all_reviews.title: Pyramids (Discworld, #7)
all_reviews.author: Terry Pratchett

all_reviews.title: Cat’s Cradle
all_reviews.author: Kurt Vonnegut Jr.

all_reviews.title: Nutshell
all_reviews.author: Ian McEwan
all_reviews.review:
Creative, funny, and replete with satisfying callouts to a different work (won't spoil the fun by naming it). This book was a pleasure to read!

It was the first book by Ian McEwan I've read, but it won't be the last.

all_reviews.title: The Goldfinch
all_reviews.author: Donna Tartt
all_reviews.review:
I can't remember the last time I gave up on a book...

The Goldfinch did not engage me in any way - not the language, not the plot, not the characters. I want my two weeks of reading back.

Grim, horrifying, hopeless...

For best results, read alone in a semi-lit room.

all_reviews.review: Not as fun and wonderful as The Colour of Magic, but still a good fix of Discworld antics. Worth a read if you enjoyed following Rincewind and Twoflowers in book 1.

recent_reviews.title: Wild Dark Shore
recent_reviews.author: Charlotte McConaghy
recent_reviews.review:
I'm done reading climate fiction unless it's written by Richard Powers.

Alt review: it's a thin line between a good book and just a fine book (derogatory).

Double alt review: I am no hater of the Poor Communication Kills trope, but like, come on fam

recent_reviews.title: Vigil
recent_reviews.author: George Saunders
recent_reviews.review:
With recent Saunders (this and Liberation Day) I found myself craving more of the intense novelty that permeates his earlier collections. This book shares the same conceit as Lincoln in the Bardo, which is a great conceit for exploring themes on morality and humanity, but loses some of the freshness of reading about a limbo-world for the first time.

That being said, this book still delivers all the good Saunders-stuff that has him as my favorite contemporary author. He portrays his characters with a blunt sincerity that truly understands them without judgement.

Recency bias, but I found myself thinking about Joachim Trier and how his movies are filled with real, three-dimensional people instead of archetypes of good and bad. Same with Saunders (except sometimes the real people are ghosts

recent_reviews.title: The Correspondent
recent_reviews.author: Virginia      Evans
recent_reviews.review: Easy reading, creative structure, well-wrought main character, funny at points, sad at points, what is not to like here??

recent_reviews.title: Slouching Towards Bethlehem
recent_reviews.author: Joan Didion
recent_reviews.review: Candidly, I struggle with collections of essays (which is surprising because I love collections of short stories). Not doing myself any favors by reading this late at night, I would sometimes catch my eyes just scanning the words. However, for the essays that caught me, and there were quite a few, I was loving the prose and structure. In particular, "On Keeping a Notebook" and "Goodbye to All That" resonated deeply and personally (having kept a journal for years, and having had a dreamlike 6 months of living in nyc in my early twenties).

recent_reviews.title: Harry Potter and the Sorcerer's Stone (Harry Potter, #1)
recent_reviews.author: J.K. Rowling
recent_reviews.review:
Full-cast Dolby audiobook for commuting / solo workouts.

This was a really fun new addition to the many many many ways to consume the Harry Potter world. Shall I list them all? The original books, the Jim Dale audiobooks, the Stephen Fry audiobooks, the movies, dramione smut, the old PC video games, the newer Switch video game, the future HBO series.

And now this, a full-cast audiobook with mind-boggling 3D sound effects and great voice acting (some of whom you will recognize, Matthew Macfadyen so good!)

recent_reviews.title: Pale Fire
recent_reviews.author: Vladimir Nabokov
recent_reviews.review:
Wow this was l i t e r a r y, and it was just at the outer limit of my attention span outside of an academic setting (don't get many of those these days do I). I was only able to get through thanks to the long break from work and work related stress. I'm glad I had the chance because in the end this was a very rewarding puzzle to unpack and enjoy.

At some points I wished to have this in print format for easier flipping back and forth, but I appreciated Kindle's X-Ray feature for the first time ever, and I very much relied on its dictionary (was looking up about one word per page).

With all the cross-references and intertextual links, by the time I finished I felt like I'd read the entire book three times.

recent_reviews.title: Barbarians at the Gate: The Fall of RJR Nabisco
recent_reviews.author: Bryan Burrough
recent_reviews.review: Just like Succession except it’s a 600 page book and it all actually happened
